_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Cumberland County, NJ. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Cumberland was created from Salem County in 1748. Records are generally well-preserved, with glass industry and oyster fleet records supplementing civil documentation.
display_name: Cumberland County, New Jersey — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Bridgeton, NJ — Broad Street (with modern additions)

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the 1848 building. No significant record loss documented.

record_losses.cause: County creation — Cumberland carved from Salem County in 1748
record_losses.scope: structural — pre-1748 records in Salem County
record_losses.description: Cumberland County records begin at county formation in 1748. Pre-1748 records for the area are in Salem County at Salem City. No major fire or disaster has destroyed Cumberland County courthouse records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in what became Cumberland County before 1748, the records are in Salem County. This applies to the earliest colonial families along the Cohansey and Maurice Rivers.

deeds.completeness: Complete from county formation. Pre-1748 in Salem County.
deeds.repository: Cumberland County Clerk's Office, Bridgeton; NJ State Archives

wills_estates.gaps: Some early loose papers in poor condition.
wills_estates.repository: Cumberland County Surrogate's Office; NJ State Archives

court_minutes.gaps: Minor condition issues with earliest volumes.
court_minutes.repository: Cumberland County Clerk's Office; NJ State Archives

vital_records.repository: NJ State Archives; NJ Department of Health

substitute_sources.description: Bridgeton, Millville, and other glass factories maintained employment records, production logs, and store ledgers. These document the skilled glass blowers and factory workers who were the backbone of Cumberland County's industrial economy.
substitute_sources.repository: Cumberland County Historical Society, Bridgeton; Wheaton Arts and Cultural Center, Millville; NJ State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Important for glass-worker families. Employment records identify workers by name, skill level, and sometimes origin.
substitute_sources.source: Pepper, The Glass Gaffers of New Jersey (1971).

substitute_sources.description: The Delaware Bay oyster industry at Port Norris and Bivalve generated boat registration, licensing, and employment records. The Bayshore Center at Bivalve maintains historical collections.
substitute_sources.repository: Bayshore Center at Bivalve; Cumberland County Historical Society; NJ Division of Fish and Wildlife historical records
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for families in the oyster industry. Boat registrations name owners and operators. Shucking house employment records identify workers.
substitute_sources.source: Hall, Daily Life in the Delaware Bay Oyster Industry (1988).

substitute_sources.description: The Alliance Colony (1882) and Rosenhayn Colony near Vineland were among the first Jewish agricultural settlements in America. The Jewish Agricultural Society maintained records of settlers, land purchases, and community life.
substitute_sources.repository: American Jewish Historical Society; Alliance Colony Heritage Association; Cumberland County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for Jewish families at Alliance, Rosenhayn, and Norma. Colony records document families by origin, occupation, and land purchase.

narrative_hooks: Cumberland County's records begin in 1748 — before that, look in Salem County. If {ancestor_name} lived along the Cohansey before 1748, the deed is at Salem.

For glass-worker families, the factory records tell the story the courthouse cannot — {ancestor_name}'s skill level, wages, and working conditions documented in ledgers that survived because the glass industry kept meticulous accounts.
The oyster fleet at Port Norris left records in boat registrations and shucking-house payrolls — if {ancestor_name} worked the Delaware Bay oysters, the evidence is at the Bayshore Center.

The Jewish colonies at Alliance and Rosenhayn have their own archive — if {ancestor_name} came to Cumberland County through the Jewish Agricultural Society, the colony records document the settlement in detail.
source_references: Cumberland County Clerk's Office. Guide to Cumberland County Records.
Pepper, Adeline. The Glass Gaffers of New Jersey. New York: Charles Scribner's Sons, 1971.
Hall, John W. Daily Life in the Delaware Bay Oyster Industry. Bridgeton: Cumberland County Historical Society, 1988.
Brandes, Joseph. Immigrants to Freedom. Philadelphia: University of Pennsylvania Press, 1971.
